text: The surgical team positions the robotic surgery ports, utilizing the patient's prior incisions to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon gains access to the abdomen using an optical trocar technique, with the trocar visibly penetrating the abdominal wall. A transversus abdominis plane block is performed for regional analgesia, likely involving the injection of anesthetic near the transversus abdominis muscle.
Robotic ports are placed, followed by a brief laparoscopic lysis of adhesions to create sufficient working space. The robotic system is then docked for the procedure.

Robotic instruments are inserted, and the surgeon performs an extensive lysis of adhesions in the right upper quadrant. Dense adhesions between the liver and gallbladder are taken down to expose these structures. Sharp dissection is used to separate dense adhesions between the liver and the duodenum.
The surgeon carefully lifts the liver and removes adhesions connecting it to the omentum, with the hepatic flexure of the colon visible immediately beneath.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to start lateral dissection, carefully taking down adhesions in the right upper quadrant to expose the liver and gallbladder.

The surgeon performs a port hop maneuver, repositioning the camera to arm three while two graspers are positioned to the left. Using near-infrared light on the robotic camera, the surgeon begins to separate the omentum from the exposed gallbladder plate, taking advantage of preoperative indocyanine green administration.

The gallbladder gradually comes into view as the surgeon utilizes near-infrared imaging with indocyanine green to highlight structures containing bile, including the duodenum, gallbladder, and biliary tree.

The surgeon creates a plane between the gallbladder and the duodenum using sharp dissection. The gallbladder is elevated, providing exposure to the angle of Calot. Dissection begins laterally at the angle of Calot, which is considered the safest starting point.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the gallbladder adjacent to the liver on both sides, employing the extended critical view of safety technique. Near-infrared light and cyanine green are used to facilitate the identification of the biliary ductal anatomy, with the angle of Calot being developed.

The surgeon creates an incision along the cystic duct, identifying the anterior and posterior branches of the cystic artery. The absence of bile in these structures is confirmed using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging, achieving a critical view of safety.

The surgeon verifies the dissection from the lateral side using the doublet view technique, confirmed with indocyanine green (ICG). The cystic arteries are transected first, creating additional space for the upcoming radiographic cholangiogram.

A surgical clip is applied to the gallbladder, and a cholangiogram catheter is introduced. A ductotomy is performed, and the catheter is secured in place. The initial cholangiogram shows no filling of the hepatic ducts or evidence of choledocholithiasis.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver using a domed down approach, employing near-infrared light and indocyanine green (ICG) to maintain the dissection plane through dense inflammation. This technique is used to better align the ductal anatomy for a second cholangiogram.

The gallbladder is completely removed from the liver bed. Fluorescence cholangiography is used to confirm the ductal anatomy. A suture secures the cholangiogram catheter due to the short cystic duct. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg and administered morphine to facilitate the next cholangiogram.

The completion cholangiogram shows complete filling of the right and left hepatic ducts and biliary radicals. The surgeon removes the cholangiogram catheter and uses a suture to ligate the cystic duct.

Two locking clips are placed on the side of the common bile duct using fluorescence cholangiography to ensure proper placement without narrowing the duct. The first clip is removed, and the gallbladder is placed into a specimen bag and extracted from the abdomen.
